info.title: Benchmark Validity Assessment Stage 3 (expert_3fcd1181e6a6@anonymous.invalid)
info.description:
Thank you for participating in this expert validation study.

You will review automated validity assessments for benchmark datasets in specific deployment contexts. For each assessment, you will:

1. Read a companion PDF with the detailed analysis
2. Answer questions about the assessment's accuracy and completeness

Each section includes a link to the relevant PDF — please open it before answering the questions.

Estimated time: 2.5 - 5 hours.

IMPORTANT: The form has 34 sections. Please aim to take 5-10 minutes on each one.

Please be as transparent as possible with your answers. This will help us accurately frame any contributions or uses of the pipeline in the paper, as well as help us improve the pipeline for future iterations.

IMPORTANT: The total PDF materials are quite long due to the inclusion of detailed evidence tables. You should aim to fully digest the main text of each document, but you do not need to exhaustively read the evidence tables — they are provided as a reference to help you assess your answers to the questions in this form.

Your responses are saved automatically as you progress (requires being signed into your Google account; drafts are tied to your browser and device).

items.title: Assessment 1: CRISISLTLSUM — Ecuador — Humanitarian Crisis Social Media Analysts
items.description:
Use case and domain: Timeline generation for coordination and evaluation of response efforts across time published in social media. The data would be tweets from natural disaster domain generated in Ecuadorian Spanish.
Target population: Latin American data and social analysts working at NGO and international organizations (e.g. Red Cross, UN OCHA) who seek to find how affected populations' needs evolve across time. They would be fluent in American English and Spanish, potentially speakers of different accents of Latin American Spanish.

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "CrisisLTLSum covers four crisis categories: wildfires, local fires, traffic incidents, and storms. For your Ecuador-focused deployment, are there disaster types your users would prioritize that fall outside this set — for example, volcanic eruptions (Cotopaxi, Tungurahua), landslides triggered by La Niña events, floods in coastal regions like Manabí, or earthquake aftershock sequences? Which crisis types matter most to Red Cross and UN OCHA coordinators in your context?"
  → "Definitively, there are other types of event that would not be covered. They focus on social and humanitarian crisis, those that affect a larger number of population. Specially when the impact exceeds what the local effort can cover"

Q2 (IC): "The benchmark tweets reference US-specific geographic markers (street names, county identifiers, US emergency agency handles like FEMA or local fire departments). In your deployment, location signals in tweets would reference Ecuadorian administrative units (parroquias, cantones, provincias), local emergency agencies (SGR, Cuerpo de Bomberos), and landmarks familiar to Ecuadorian users. Would your analysts expect the system to correctly anchor events to these local geographic and institutional references, or is rough regional-level geolocation sufficient?"
  → "The analyst would expect the system to achor events to those Ecuador specific entities"

Q3 (OC): "The benchmark's ground-truth relevance labels and summaries were produced for US English-language tweets by annotators operating in a US crisis-response context. For your use case, relevance judgments may differ: a tweet about road access to a rural community, or an informal community radio broadcast transcribed into Twitter, might carry high operational value for an NGO coordinator but low value by US annotation standards. Who in your organization would serve as the authoritative judge of tweet relevance and summary quality — field coordinators, social analysts, or affected-community representatives — and do you have access to them for validation?"
  → "A inter-operational team would be in charge of judgment, not only someone with an specific role (a local field coordinator, a social analyst, a domain-expert analyst)"

Q4 (IF): "The benchmark is built entirely from English-language tweets. Your deployment targets Ecuadorian Spanish, which may include code-switching with Kichwa or other indigenous languages, regional slang, informal abbreviations common in disaster contexts (e.g., 'damnificados', 'albergue', 'socorristas'), and non-standard orthography in crisis conditions. Would the tweet stream your system processes be predominantly standard written Spanish, or do you expect significant informal register, dialect variation, or indigenous-language mixing that a system trained on English social media might not handle?"
  → "The predominant would be Ecuadorian Spanish, possibly with some local entities names in those local languages. I would expect a mix of informal and semi formal register. So yes, if the system is trained only on English social media (and potentially fine-tuned on local Spanish) it would probably miss local characteristics of Ecuadorian social media data."

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/15PuSQ1HY7DzplsILyRpBEbM3Qq8xS8MO/view?usp=drivesdk

items.title: Are there specific refinements or additions that would make this assessment more useful for practitioners in your domain?
Assessment 2: CRISISLTLSUM — Mexico City and Central America Bilingual Crisis Journalism
items.description:
Use case and domain: Summarization of quick messages (e.g. tweets) for event reporting in the crisis domain for news writing in American English and Mexican Spanish mostly
Target population: Journalists and media workers who monitor social media to cover breaking news from Mexico City. They would speak Mexican Spanish natively (possible neutral/standard regional variety), and English fluently. They would be expected to use the model in English and Spanish to cover news of disasters happening not only in Mexico, but also south of the United States and also Central America.

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "The benchmark covers four crisis types: wildfires, local fires, traffic incidents, and storms — all drawn from US contexts. For your journalists covering Mexico City, Central America, and the US-Mexico border region, are there crisis types that matter but likely fall outside those four categories — for example, volcanic activity (Popocatépetl), earthquakes, flooding from tropical systems, or civil unrest? And within the shared categories like storms, do the local sub-types (e.g., hurricane landfalls on Pacific vs Gulf coasts of Mexico) differ meaningfully from US storm patterns your journalists would need covered?"
  → "Yes, there are and the examples mentioned are good examples. Possible the context in which these shared crisis type occur are quite different and the type of damage, but not the type of event itself."

Q2 (IC): "The benchmark's crisis events are tied to specific US locations — named streets, buildings, and counties. For your journalists covering Mexican and Central American crises, how place-specific does the system need to be? For instance, would it need to correctly identify and contextualize references to colonias, municipios, delegaciones (like Iztapalapa or Tlalpan in CDMX), or regional landmarks that would appear in Spanish-language social media posts but not in US-sourced training data?"
  → "This is a key part of the evaluation system, it should be able to identify correctly location and time references, grouping and aggregating along with the specific context associated with the event."

Q3 (OC): "The benchmark's human-written summaries were produced by annotators working from US English Twitter data. For your journalists, what counts as a good crisis summary may differ — for example, the level of official source attribution expected, how casualty figures are framed, or how community impact is described in Mexican media conventions. Do your journalists follow a specific editorial style (e.g., AP Español, local outlet guidelines) that defines what a 'good' summary looks like, and would that diverge from US news writing norms?"
  → "I agree with this concern. Even in the same language, the data that is considered relevant to be included in the summary depends on the user profile. Yes, my journalists follow a particular editorial style. However, part of their job should be to adapt the summary to their needs, a next layer would be necessary for a direct use. In other words, it would be better for the summary to be less restrictive and cover more in case of uncertainty."

Q4 (OF): "The benchmark produces abstractive summaries as free-form text. For your journalists' actual workflow, do they need the system to output in both English and Spanish, or would they typically receive an English-language summary and translate/adapt it themselves? If bilingual output is needed, would the Spanish output need to match Mexican journalistic register specifically, or would a neutral Latin American Spanish be acceptable?"
  → "A bilingual output would be needed. I would expect higher quality of summaries if the models outputs English summaries in addition to the Spanish version, which would need to be revised and adapted to the journalistic register and required reporting format."

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1qieAUTRB8-zWNQLaU9pVmyAt1_Ftgsz-/view?usp=drivesdk

items.title: Are there specific refinements or additions that would make this assessment more useful for practitioners in your domain?
Comparative 1: CRISISLTLSUM vs. CNN/DAILYMAIL
items.description:
The pipeline assessed two benchmarks for the same deployment context: CRISISLTLSUM (a benchmark proposed by you) and CNN/DAILYMAIL (https://arxiv.org/abs/1506.03340) (a benchmark we selected for comparison). Both were evaluated across the same six validity dimensions. The comparative PDF below presents excerpts from each benchmark's individual assessment for this context — please review it before answering the questions.

CRISISLTLSUM: 1.3/5 avg | CNN/DAILYMAIL: 1.0/5 avg
Context: Ecuador — Humanitarian Crisis Social Media Analysts

▶ Open the Comparative PDF: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1kv527aWKT_0uhnuuP_Jr90wArtZJn7nv/view?usp=drivesdk

Assessment 3: WXIMPACTBENCH — Argentine Government Disaster Reporting Reliability — Policy Staff
items.description:
Use case and domain: Use case: Reliability of disaster reporting in news at national/local level, finding alignment with effective and verified information (e.g. noisy vs real signals). The evaluation data could report or not disaster damage.
Target population: Policy makers and government agencies in Argentina studying trustiness and reliability of disaster communication across different channels are (e.g news, social media post, press). They would be based in Buenos Aires, speakers of Rioplatense Spanish, with an intermediate to advanced level of English, at least a master's degree, and be part of an upper-middle-class household.

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "The benchmark categories cover infrastructural, political, financial, ecological, agricultural, and human health impacts of weather events. For disaster reporting reliability in Argentina, are there impact categories that matter to your policy makers but might not appear here — for example, impacts on informal settlements (villas), agricultural disruption in Pampa or Patagonia, or flooding along the Río de la Plata basin? Are there disaster types (e.g., pampero windstorms, ENSO-driven droughts, urban flash floods in Buenos Aires) whose reporting patterns your users need to evaluate?"
  → "No, the examples provided could use the labels from the benchmark. But it is true that sub label or categories would be very interesting to have."

Q2 (IC): "The benchmark is built from historical Canadian newspaper articles, which reflect Northern Hemisphere disaster framing, infrastructure norms, and institutional actors. For your Argentine policy makers assessing reporting reliability, would culturally embedded assumptions in the data — such as which agencies are cited as authoritative, what counts as 'normal' disaster response, or how agricultural loss is framed — misalign with what Argentine news sources or government communications typically convey?"
  → "I think it depends on the type of data. I would not expect concerning assumptions in press and new articles but I do think social media information or other type of informal data to convey meaning not represented in the benchmark"

Q3 (OO): "Your deployment centers on distinguishing noisy or unreliable signals from verified disaster reporting — a reliability or trustworthiness judgment — rather than simply classifying impact type. The benchmark's output space is multi-label impact classification and ranking. Does your use case require a scoring dimension for source credibility, misinformation likelihood, or signal-to-noise quality, which may not map onto the existing six impact labels or the ranking task?"
  → "Th ideal use case would need a scoring dimension for source credibility, however I believe this could be built upon the outputs of the model in a way that user can verify/aggregate model's results, considering other external factors and metadata to score it (such as the relevance/popularity of the source)"

Q4 (OC): "The benchmark's ground-truth labels were derived from Canadian newspaper content, likely annotated by annotators familiar with North American disaster communication norms. For your context, would Argentine policy experts, emergency management officials, or journalists plausibly disagree with those labels — for instance, on whether a given report constitutes a 'real signal' of disaster impact versus noise — especially given differences in how Argentine media covers government response or social vulnerability?"
  → "I believe in the Argentinian context there are less established norms of crisis communication (the public space is more fluctuant than in Canada/North America). Probably policy experts and emergency officials get inspired in those communication standards developed at international organizations or in the Global North, to later adapt them to the local context. I believe the use of models and identifying how they fail could potentially help to define more specific labels/sub labels. I also think local press tend to follow patterns from those areas, so I don't think local users would disagree with labels applied to the Canadian data"

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1kwFGPV5RvSVGuiELss2oMYFUn_VzOlEu/view?usp=drivesdk

Assessment 4: WXIMPACTBENCH — Gran Chaco Tri-Border Emergency Response Zone
items.description:
Use case and domain: Use Case: Early identification of damage type caused by a disaster and reported in local news and social media posts from Northwest Argentina, Paraguay and South Bolivia (e.g. Gran Chaco regions).
Target population: Target population: National emergency responders, gendarmerie, civil forces, technical practitioners in public emergency sector from Argentina, Bolivia or Paraguay. They all would speak and write in Spanish, Andean variety, and would have an intermediate level of English.

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "The benchmark was built around Canadian historical newspaper content about weather impacts. Your deployment covers Gran Chaco disaster events — which may include flooding from the Pilcomayo and Bermejo rivers, droughts, locust outbreaks, chaco fires, or infrastructure failures in isolated rural areas. Do these event types and their local consequences (e.g., disrupted cattle routes, damage to dirt-road access to indigenous communities, cross-border displacement) map onto the impact categories your responders care about, or are there Gran Chaco-specific damage types that would be missing?"
  → "The types of event map but only up to certain point. For example flooding and fires are common for both areas and I'd expect the benchmark to help me to see how well models can capture general damage type and in description. If translated, I could also see how this performance is lost. But the reality of the region can be quite different. I'd expect responders to focus more on the international borders and strategic economic routes since those are the ones who would mainly reported in the news. Bu with regards of social media, I do believe this benchmark would be quite limited to represent how well a model understands about the regional socioeconomic context."

Q2 (IC): "The benchmark was constructed from Canadian English-language newspapers. Your source texts are Spanish-language local news and social media from Argentina, Bolivia, and Paraguay — including informal writing, regional slang, code-switching with indigenous languages (Guaraní, Quechua, Wichí), and platform-specific abbreviations. Would your responders consider damage reports written in these registers as representative of what the system needs to handle correctly?"
  → "Partially yes. I would expect the system to be used to analyze content in regional Spanish, mainly Rio Platense Spanish, that contains regional vocabulary (e.g. location and names in those indigenous languages for example)."

Q3 (OC): "The benchmark's impact labels (infrastructural, political, financial, ecological, agricultural, human health) were validated against Canadian newspaper content. For your responders in the Gran Chaco, would the boundary between these categories feel natural — for example, would a flooded dirt road that cuts off an indigenous community be classified as infrastructural, ecological, or human health? And who among your users — field gendarmes, civil protection technicians, national coordinators — should be the authoritative judge of correct label assignment in your context?"
  → "A flooded dirt road would need to be severe enough to appear in the news or social media, affecting semi-urban areas and more rural areas as well. I don't expect only a road affecting an indigenous community area would be the case. I think the label for this scenario would depend on how the consequences are framed. For example, humans lost, inhabitants without water neither food supplies for an extended period of time, all routes in the area affected preventing commercial trucks' mobility.
National coordinators would be the ones assigned to label"

Q4 (OO): "The benchmark outputs are multi-label classifications and ranked answers. Your responders need to quickly identify damage types from incoming news and social media. Does your system need to produce a ranked list of impact types, a simple set of simultaneous labels, a confidence score, or a structured alert that routes information to specific response units? Would the same output format serve a field gendarme in Salta and a national emergency coordinator in Buenos Aires equally well?"
  → "The system would need to produce simultaneous labels of impact types at the minimum. I wouldn't expect the system to be good enough to rank the local data accurate enough to relied directly on the system output without human intervention/check. The system output format would serve differently for those users. For the gendarme, a fast iteration could facilitate to find information for particular event happening in an ongoing disaster (e.g. in a flood, a gas truck, a school bus stuck in summer with high temperatures). For a national coordinator in Buenos Aires, the system output would be useful at scale for decision-making, for example to asses the national needs and allocate limited national and public resources (e.g. water bombing helicopter, equipped and trained emergency rescue)."

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Fl1g21q0EZ5Xj2ly5LNZiVpwcugOPeQy/view?usp=drivesdk

Comparative 2: WXIMPACTBENCH vs. CRISISBENCH
items.description:
The pipeline assessed two benchmarks for the same deployment context: WXIMPACTBENCH (a benchmark proposed by you) and CRISISBENCH (https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/ICWSM/article/view/18115) (a benchmark we selected for comparison). Both were evaluated across the same six validity dimensions. The comparative PDF below presents excerpts from each benchmark's individual assessment for this context — please review it before answering the questions.

WXIMPACTBENCH: 2.2/5 avg | CRISISBENCH: 2.3/5 avg
Context: Argentine Government Disaster Reporting Reliability — Policy Staff

▶ Open the Comparative PDF: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1XMWouODMco-PTr7pWPBlEkhz0qHHm_bo/view?usp=drivesdk
